figures.caption: Figure D.2: Fits on a star-shaped dataset and a moon-shaped dataset, respectively, for varying levels of λ

Figure 3.3: Visual illustration of Theorem 3.4. The red curve represents the manifold underlying a point cloud generated according to the data-generating mechanism described in Appendix F.2. For each sample size N , we repeat the following procedure 100 times: (i) generate a sample of size N using the datagenerating mechanism described in Appendix F.2; and (ii) apply the PA algorithm and plot the resulting estimate as a blue curve.

Figure 3.1: The star- and cashew-shaped point clouds in the first and second rows are generated via the mechanisms described in Appendices F.3 and F.4, respectively. We apply the PA algorithm to the point clouds. The top row is initialized using only spherical normalization, and the bottom row using ISOMAP followed by spherical normalization (see Appendix C.1 for details). Each column displays the fitting results corresponding to a prespecified value of λ. This figure shows that a fitted closed surface gradually shrinks to the center of a point cloud as λ approaches ∞, as stated in Theorem 3.2.

Figure D.3: (i): Plot of geodesic distance in the M = [0, 1] case between f∗ N,λ and f∗ N,∞ (PCA-line) as a function of λ, where each line corresponds to a different N . (ii) and (iii): L2 distance between f∗ N,λ and f∗ N,∞ (sample mean) as a function of λ for the M = S1 and M = S2 cases, respectively.

Figure 3.2: Illustration of the PA algorithm and the role of Assumption 3. The vertical axis denotes the objective Lλ(f) and the horizontal axis represents the function space F (P) (illustration only, not to scale). The region U (green bracket; boundaries indicated by dashed vertical lines) is a “basin of attraction” in which the updating operator Tλ is intended to operate. Starting from an initialization f (0) λ ∈ U , successive iterations f (n+1)

Figure C.2: The left figure depicts the original dataset. The right panel shows the point cloud of the corresponding ISOMAP into 2 dimensions. The color of the plot denotes the data’s original ordering. Observe how the ordering along the curve is preserved.

Figure C.1: A latent manifold M with approximate center at the black point. Taking the ordering counterclockwise along the manifold, we have the ordering (p1, p2, p3). Now let θ denote the angle along the horizontal axis to a point on the manifold, and θij denote the distance between pi and pj . This way, we see that θ13 > θ23, implying that the circular projection method believes p2 and p3 are closer together than p1 and p3 are, which is incorrect.
Figure C.3: First and Converged estimates of data where the initialization is either through ISOMAP into a circular projection, or just a circular projection on raw data.

Figure 2.1: The point clouds in panels (b,e) are generated via the mechanisms described in Appendices F.1 and F.2, respectively. The persistent diagrams (PDs; Fasy et al., 2014) corresponding to these two point clouds are shown in panels (a,d), respectively. The significant homology features in the PDs correctly identify the topologies of the manifolds underlying the point clouds. The curves displayed in panels (b,e) are fitted using the PA algorithm (Algorithm C.1) and correspond to an excessively small, moderate, and excessively large λ value. These numerical results validate Theorem 3.2. Note that the moderate value of λ is not optimal. The optimal choice of λ is described in Section 5 and indicated in Figure 5.1. Panels (c,f) display the nonincreasing values of the cost functional {LN,λ(f (n) N,λ)}n∈N, where the penalty term ∥∇2f (n) N,λ∥2L2(M) is computed using Lemma B.3. Theorem 3.3 implies that LN,λ(f (n) N,λ) converges to LN,λ(f ∗ N,λ), as the number of iterations n→∞, under the regularity conditions specified in Theorem 3.3, where f∗ N,λ = argminf∈F(PN ) LN,λ(f) and PN := 1 N ∑N i=1 δXi .
Figure D.1: Fits on rotated sinusoidal and helix datasets in ambient R3 space for various values of λ.

Figure C.4: (i) Underlying manifold (red solid curve) displayed alongside the first spline fit with projection indices from direct 1d ISOMAP initialization (blue dotted curve). (ii) Here the spline fit is instead procured via projection indices from 2d ISOMAP followed by circular projection.

Figure 1.1: A curve f(I) (black) fitted to a point cloud (orange), represented as the image of a function f : I→ RD. For an observation X, the point Πf (X) (red) is its nearest-point projection onto the curve, and πf (X) is the projection index defined in Section 2.3. The residual X−Πf (X) (blue) is perpendicular to the curve at the projection Πf (X) (right-angle marker), and the brace indicates the error ∥X−Πf (X)∥.
